title: External Cardiac Pacing (Zoll X Series)
content_markdown:
#### Indications
- Bradycardia with poor perfusion. 

#### Procedure

- Ensure chest leads are positioned well off the chest. 
- Remove CPR feedback pad from the posterior pad (red rectangle) (see **Figure 1**).

Figure 1

- Place posterior pad vertically beneath scapula, along left vertebral edge (see **Figure 2**).

Figure 2

Figure 3

- Place anterior pad (purple circle) horizontally in V2/V3 position (see **Figure 3**).

- Place CPR feedback pad in centre of the chest on the sternum. 
- Attach pads to pads cable.

- Push Green PACER button.

- Select [PACER RATE] to at least 50% above the patient’s intrinsic rate.

- Select [PACER OUTPUT] and reduce to 10mA.

- Select [START PACING].

- Ensure pacing markers are visible on ECG.

- Slowly increase electrical current by 10mA increments until electrical and mechanical capture are achieved.

- Once electrical and mechanical capture are obtained (change in QRS morphology and pacing markers synchronised with each QRS complex) increase out by a further 10mA and then decrease by 5mA.

- Confirm capture with manual pulse rate and BP.

- Provide analgesia (morphine/ fentanyl/ midazolam) if required.

#### Contraindications
- Nil when indicated.

#### Complications
- Pain – provide reassurance and appropriate pain relief (morphine/fentanyl/midazolam).
- Failure to capture due to resistance or unknown reason.

#### Specific Notes
- External cardiac pacing is preferred over adrenaline infusion for bradycardia post cardiac arrest.
- If IV access in situ, Adrenaline infusion must also be prepared for use in event of pacing failure.

Morphine.text: Morphine: 10mg IV or SC Midazolam: 5mg IV or 10mg SC. Repeat @10/60 (max. dose 60mg)
Calculated dose of morphine above 10 mg Doses above 10 mg are rarely required.
Calculated dose of morphine above 20 mg
IV/IO morphine may be administered to patients with mild-moderate hypotension (sBP 70 – 90mmHg) 0.025mg/kg IV/IO over 60 seconds.
Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 3mL (30mg) morphine and 6mL (30mg) midazolam to make 30mg:30mg/30mL
Consider: Adjunct 0.05mg/kg bolus of morphine for patients with severely painful injuries to achieve adequate analgesia.
Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 6mL (30mg) midazolam and 3mL (30mg) morphine to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

. If dilution of the drug is required, open the normal saline ampoule or insert the needle and syringe into the injection port of a bag of saline. Draw back on the syringe and draw up the required amount of fluid for dilution of the drug (i.e. 9mls of normal saline with 1ml of Morphine Sulphate for a total volume of 10mls).

##### Type
Opioid analgesic [S8]

##### Presentations
10mg in 1ml – glass ampoule

##### Actions

- Analgesia

- Anxiolytic

- Vasodilation

##### Pharmacokinetics
**IV**
 Onset: 5 - 10 minutes
 Max Effect: 20 minutes
 Duration: 4 - 5 hours

**IM**
 Onset: 10 - 30 minutes
 Max Effect: 30 minutes
 Duration: 4 - 5 hours

#### Uses

- Analgesia for moderate to severe pain

- Maintain analgosedation in intubated patients in conjunction with midazolam.

- Reduce dyspnoea in palliative care patients.

- Relieve suffering due to terminal palliative care emergencies (no active treatment).

#### Adverse Effects

- Nausea and vomiting

- Altered level of consciousness

- Respiratory depression

- Hypotension

- Bradycardia

#### Contraindications

- Respiratory depression

- Systolic blood pressure Disregard contraindications when managing palliative care emergencies (no active treatment) patients.

#### Precautions

- GCS ≤13

- Known hypersensitivity

- Frail patients

- COPD patients

- Hypovolaemic patients

- Systolic BP 70 – 90mmHg (see below)

- Children **AP:** max. total dose 20mg
- **ICP:** no max. total dose
- Consider IM: 
- IV/IO unobtainable
- Little risk of hypotension- Patient contact estimated >20/60- No ischaemic chest pain- 0.1mg/kg IM 
- Repeat once @30/60 if required
- **AP:** max. total dose 20mg##### Adults only
IV/IO morphine may be administered to patients with mild-moderate hypotension (sBP 70 – 90mmHg)

- 0.025mg/kg IV/IO over 60 seconds. 
- Repeat @10/60 as required.
- Do not repeat if sBP drops ≥10mmHg, even if it remains above 70mmHg. 
- **AP:** max. total dose 20mg- **ICP:** no max. total dose#### Post-intubation analgosedation (as adjunct to midazolam)##### Adult (infusion pump)
**Note:** Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 3mL (30mg) morphine and 6mL (30mg) midazolam to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

- **ICP: **Initial infusion rate: 5mg/hr 
- Lower soft infusion limit: 1mg/hr
- Upper soft infusion limit: 30mg/hr
- Upper hard infusion limit: 50mg/hr- **ICP: **Default bolus: 2mg (2mL) 
- Lower hard bolus limit: 0.5mg (0.5mL)
- Upper hard bolus limit: 5mg (5mL)#### Palliative care
**Note:** Always refer to and follow patient specific treatment plan when available.
##### Adult with dyspnoea
- 2.5 - 5mg SC/IM/IV
Consider patient weight when selecting dose
##### Adult with distressing pain
- Refer to Palliative Care Medication Calculator
##### Adult with terminal palliative care emergency
- SC/IM/IV: 10mg
In conjunction with midazolam
##### Paediatric
Refer to patient specific treatment plan for all palliative care presentations.

Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 3mL (30mg) morphine and 6mL (30mg) midazolam to make 30mg:30mg/30mL
Up to 4mg/kg IM injection
Consider: Adjunct 0.05mg/kg boluses of midazolam with ketamine infusions/boluses to achieve adequate sedation.
It is permissible to concurrently administer Midazolam and Levetiracetam if a seizure has persisted ≥20/60 without any pharmacological management prior to ACTAS arrival.
If patient has previously had an unpleasant (fearful) experience with adenosine, consider a pre-dose of midazolam 1 – 1.5mg IV.
0.2mg/kg IM (max. 10mg)
Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 6mL (30mg) midazolam and 3mL (30mg) morphine to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

##### Type
Benzodiazepine 

##### Presentations
15mg in 3mL ampoule

##### Actions

- Antiepileptic

- Sedative

- Anxiolytic

- Amnesic

##### Pharmacokinetics
**IV/IO**
 Onset: 2 minutes
 Max Effect: 3 - 5 minutes
 Duration: 7 - 75 minutes

**IM**
 Onset: 5 - 15 minutes
 Max Effect: 30 - 60 minutes
 Duration: 2 - 6 hours

#### Uses

- Status epilepticus

- Post intubation analgosedation in conjunction with morphine

- Relieve distress or restlessness in the palliative care patient

- Relieve suffering due to terminal palliative care emergencies (no active treatment)

#### Adverse Effects

- Hypotension
More common when administer via fast IV push

- Respiratory depression and loss of airway
More common at high doses or when synergising with other CNS depressant substances

#### Contraindications

- Previous allergic reaction to Midazolam

#### Precautions

- Hepatic failure

- Frail or elderly

- Known or suspected concurrent CNS depressant use

- Known or suspected haemodynamic compromise

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- **Approved** for administration in ACTAS setting.

##### Breastfeeding
- **Approved** for administration in ACTAS setting.

#### Doses#### Status Epilepticus##### Adult and Paediatric
- 0.2mg/kg IM (max. 10mg) 
- Repeat @5/60 if required. (max. 2 doses)

- **ICP:** 0.2mg/kg IV/IO push over 60 sec. (max. 10mg) 
- Repeat @5/60 if required. (max. 2 doses)
#### Post Intubation Sedation (as adjunct to ketamine)##### Adult and Paediatric
- **ICP:** 0.05mg/kg IV/IO push over 60 sec. 
- Repeat @5/60 as required.

#### Post Intubation Sedation (as adjunct to morphine)##### Adult
**Note:** Withdraw 29mL from 50mL NaCl bag, then add 6mL (30mg) midazolam and 3mL (30mg) morphine to make 30mg:30mg/30mL

- **ICP:** Initial infusion rate: 5mg/hr 
- Lower soft infusion limit: 1mg/hr
- Upper soft infusion limit: 30mg/hr
- Upper hard infusion limit: 50mg/hr- **ICP:** Default bolus: 2mg (2mL) 
- Lower hard bolus limit: 0.5mg (0.5mL)
- Upper hard bolus limit: 5mg (5mL)#### Palliative Care
**Note:** Always refer to and follow patient specific treatment plan when available.
##### Adult with distress or restlessness
- 2.5 – 5mg SC/IM
Consider patient weight when selecting dose
##### Adult with terminal palliative care emergency
- 5mg SC/IM/IV
In conjunction with morphine
- Repeat @10/60 if requires (max. 60mg)

##### Paediatric
Refer to patient specific treatment plan for all palliative care presentations.

##### Type
A naturally occurring catecholamine [S3]

##### Presentations
1:10,000 – 1mg in 10ml – glass ampoule 
 1:1,000 – 1mg in 1ml – glass ampoule 

##### Actions

- \u03b1 effect: peripheral vasoconstriction

- \u03b21 effects: 

- increased rate of sinus node

- increased myocardial contractility

- increased AV conduction

- increased myocardial irritability

- \u03b22 effects: 

- bronchodilation

- vasodilation of skeletal muscle

Onset: IV = 30 seconds; IM = 30 – 90 seconds 
 Max effect: IV = 3 – 5 min; IM = 4 – 10 mins 

Endotracheal use: slightly longer times

#### Uses

- Cardiac arrest

- Anaphylaxis

- Severe life-threatening asthma

- Bradyarrhythmias resistant to atropine **- ICP only**

- Severe upper airway swelling

- Shock unresponsive to fluid boluses **- ICP only**

#### Adverse Effects

- Tachycardia

- Tachyarrhythmias

- Hypertension

#### Contraindications

- Hypovolaemic shock without adequate fluid replacement

#### Precautions
These apply to patients with cardiac output only:

- Care with patients with history of hypertension

- Care with patients with history of ischaemic heart disease

- Give extremely slowly to patients on MAO Inhibitor antidepressants (e.g. Nardil, Parnate) as adrenaline may provoke a greatly exaggerated response. Generally, patients on MAOIs with cardiac output should receive no more than \xbc of the normal dose of adrenaline, titrated to response 

- Hypothermia

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Safe to use

##### Breastfeeding
- Safe to use

#### Doses#### Bradyarrhythmias resistant to atropine##### Adult and Paediatric
- **ICP:** IV/IO adrenaline by infusion – titrated to response

#### Cardiac arrest##### Adult
- 1mg IV or IO – fast push 
- No limit on number of doses in cardiac arrest.

##### Paediatric
- IV: 0.01mg/kg – fast push 
- No limit on number of doses in cardiac arrest.

- **ICP:** IO: 0.01mg/kg – fast push 
- No limit on number of doses in cardiac arrest.
- **ICP:** ETT: NEWBORN ONLY 
- If no IV or IO access – 0.02mg/kg.

#### Anaphylaxis / asthma (severe life threatening)##### Adult
- IM: 0.5mg 
- Repeat 5 minutely (max three doses).

- **ICP:** If required – **IV/IO** adrenaline by infusion 
- Titrated to response.
##### Paediatric
- IM: 0.01mg/kg (up to 50kg) 
- Repeat 5 minutely (max three doses).

- **ICP:** If required – **IV/IO** adrenaline by infusion 
- Titrated to response.

#### Severe upper airway swelling##### Adult
- **IM:** 0.5mg 
- Repeat 5 minutely (max three doses).

##### Paediatric
- Weight >10kg: nebulise 5ml adrenaline 1:1,000
- Weight

#### Indications
- Patients requiring an adrenaline infusion.

#### Procedure

- Medication checks are to be done by two officers prior to administration.

- Adhere to all aseptic techniques.

- Inject **1mg of adrenaline into a 500ml** bag of saline via the access port.

- Fill out and attach an additive label to the fluid bag.

- Connect a giving set or a burette to the fluid bag. 
- **If you are using a giving set:** ensure the gate clamps are turned off prior to inserting into the fluid bag.
- **If you are using a burette:** connect a giving set to the bottom of the burette and ensure all gate clamps are turned off prior to inserting into the fluid bag. Now open the ventilation port then the gate clamp and fill the burette to the 100ml mark. Open the gate clamp and prime the line and then refill the burette to the 100ml mark.

- Connect the giving set to the patient and commence infusion. 
- 20 drops/minute = 1ml per minute or 2 mcg/minute
- Titrate for a response.

- Reassess respiratory and perfusion status.

#### Contraindications
- Nil when indicated.

#### Specific Notes
- Ensure that all of the delivery lines are primed with adrenaline. Failure to do this will result in a in a significant delay in the patient receiving adrenaline.
- If the adrenaline infusion is running through an IO line, you may require a pump set rather than a giving set to overcome increased intra medullar pressure.

Fentanyl.text:
##### Type
Synthetic narcotic analgesic [S8]

##### Presentations
100mcg in 2mL (5mcg/0.1mL) glass ampoule

##### Actions

- Precipitates CNS effects by binding to opioid receptors.

##### Pharmacokinetics
IN
 Onset: ~ 5 minutes
 Max Effect: 5 – 15 minutes
 Duration: ~ 60 minutes

#### Uses

- Analgesic for moderate to severe pain.

- Safe alternative analgesic in patients with known morphine allergy.

#### Adverse Effects

- Reduced conscious state

- Respiratory depression

- Nausea/vomiting

- Bradycardia (rare)

- Rigidity of the diaphragm, intercostals and jaw (rare)

#### Contraindications

- Known allergy or hypersensitivity

- Bilateral bleeding or occluded nostrils

- Age Increased risk of adverse effects. Consider reducing dose. 

- Frail or elderly
Increased risk of adverse effects. Consider reducing dose. 

- Reduced level of consciousness
Potential difficulty correctly administering via IN route. 

#### Pregnancy and Breastfeeding##### Pregnancy
- Refrain from use if labour suspected. If administered, prepare for neonatal respiratory support and naloxone administration. 

##### Breastfeeding
- May be used in conservative doses. Encourage feeding/expressing prior to administration.

#### Doses#### Analgesia – Intranasal##### Adult and Paediatric
- 1.5mcg/kg IN, up to 100mcg. 
- Repeat @5/60 intervals as required.

#### Further Information
- The atomiser dead space is 0.1mL. To account for this, an additional 0.1mL must be drawn up with the first administration. Pragmatically, this is not required when delivering the full 100mcg dose
- Ideally volume administered should be limited to 0.3mL – 0.5mL per nostril to a maximum of 1mL.
- Alternate nostrils until total dose has been delivered.
- Where possible, IN Fentanyl should be used in addition to paracetamol and an NSAID. - All patients receiving Fentanyl should be on continuous monitoring. - Fentanyl should not be administered if the maximum dose of Morphine has already been administered.
- Antiemetics should only be administered if the patient has nausea / vomiting prior to or post administration of Fentanyl.
